content.text: The tools your team uses during an incident shape how fast you resolve it. A misconfigured alert tool, a missing dashboard link, or a runbook that lives in an unlocked Confluence page no one can find under pressure — each adds minutes to time-to-mitigate. This topic defines the standard tooling stack for a GenAI production war room and the collaboration standards that make each tool effective.

attrs.alt: War room tooling stack diagram showing four layers: coordination (Slack, Zoom, PagerDuty, FireHydrant), observability (Grafana, Datadog, Loki, Jaeger), incident management (Jira, Statuspage, Incident.io), and documentation (Notion, Confluence, Git)

content.text: A functional war room needs tools in four layers. Every layer must be wired up and tested
Single point of truth for who is doing what
See what the system is doing right now
Diagnosis time drops when dashboards are pre-built
Automated escalation; external comms on schedule
Scribe can work fast from a template; post-mortem is near-automatic
All Slack configuration for incidents should be automated and consistent. Enforce these standards with a Slack bot or incident management platform:
Channel name format: #inc-YYYY-MM-DD-NNN (bot creates it automatically)
Channel topic set immediately: [SEV1 ACTIVE] <title> — IC: @name — Bridge: <link>
Required pins at T+5: bridge link, status doc, runbook, monitoring dashboard
Archive the channel 7 days after resolution — never delete it (it's the permanent record)
Set channel retention policy to minimum 90 days (post-mortem timelines reference it)
Slack Slash Commands for Incident Workflow
Custom slash commands speed up the scribe workflow and reduce format errors:

from slack_bolt import App
from datetime import datetime, timezone

app = App(token=SLACK_BOT_TOKEN, signing_secret=SLACK_SIGNING_SECRET)

@app.command("/inc-update")
def handle_update(ack, body, client):
    ack()
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc).strftime("%H:%M UTC")
    user = body["user_name"]
    text = body["text"]
    client.chat_postMessage(
        channel=body["channel_id"],
        text=f"[{now}] STATUS UPDATE ({user}): {text}",
    )

@app.command("/inc-hypothesis")
def handle_hypothesis(ack, body, client):
    ack()
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc).strftime("%H:%M UTC")
    client.chat_postMessage(
        channel=body["channel_id"],
        text=f":bulb: [{now}] HYPOTHESIS: {body['text']}",
    )

@app.command("/inc-action")
def handle_action(ack, body, client):
    """Usage: /inc-action @user <task> by <time>
    Example: /inc-action @dave cordon us-east-1a by 14:40
    """
    ack()
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc).strftime("%H:%M UTC")
    client.chat_postMessage(
        channel=body["channel_id"],
        text=f":white_check_mark: [{now}] ACTION: {body['text']}",
    )

@app.command("/inc-resolve")
def handle_resolve(ack, body, client):
    ack()
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc).strftime("%H:%M UTC")
    client.chat_postMessage(
        channel=body["channel_id"],
        text=(
            f":white_check_mark: [{now}] INCIDENT RESOLVED by {body['user_name']}\n"
            f"Resolution: {body['text']}"
        ),
    )
    client.conversations_setTopic(
        channel=body["channel_id"],
        topic=f"[RESOLVED {now}] {body['text']}",
    )
Enforce these standards for video bridge calls to reduce friction:
Joining under pressure with a password wastes 60 seconds
Off by default; IC can enable
Some incident content is legally sensitive; default off is safer
Responders must join immediately without IC approving each entry
Larger calls are unmanageable; use subcommander structure above 12
Responders need to show dashboards quickly
Subcommanders can open them on demand
The most common reason an incident takes longer than it should is that the responder has to build the query to see what is happening
the incident. Pre-built dashboards eliminate that delay.
Required Pre-Built Dashboards for GenAI Systems
P50/P95/P99 latency, error rate, request rate, token throughput
Yes — SEV1 if P99 > 10s for 5 min
Provider latency, rate limit errors (429), quota remaining
Output length distribution, refusal rate, quality score (if instrumented)
SEV2 if refusal rate spikes 3x baseline
Retrieval latency, embedding cache hit rate, vector store errors
Token spend per hour, spend rate vs budget, per-model breakdown
SEV2 if hourly spend > 150% of average
Safety filter trigger rate, PII detection events, policy violations
Pinning Dashboards to the Incident Channel
When a war room opens, the IC or bot should pin the relevant dashboard link immediately. Automate this from your incident management tool:

# FireHydrant runbook automation example
name: SEV1 Inference API
triggers:
  - type: alert
    alert_source: pagerduty
    services: [inference-api]
steps:
  - type: slack_channel_create
    name_template: "inc-{{ date }}-{{ sequence }}"
  - type: slack_pin_message
    message: |
      :chart_with_upwards_trend: Inference API Dashboard:
      https://grafana.internal/d/inference-overview
      :mag: Log search:
      https://grafana.internal/explore?ds=loki&q={job=%22inference-api%22}
      :book: Runbook:
      https://github.com/org/runbooks/blob/main/inference-api.md
  - type: page_oncall
    schedule: ml-platform-oncall
Distributed Tracing for LLM Request Diagnosis
A distributed trace for a GenAI request should capture every hop from API gateway to LLM provider. This lets responders identify exactly which component added latency:

from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.trace import SpanKind

tracer = trace.get_tracer("inference-api")

def handle_inference_request(request: InferenceRequest) -> InferenceResponse:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span(
        "inference.request",
        kind=SpanKind.SERVER,
        attributes={
            "model": request.model,
            "prompt_tokens": request.prompt_token_count,
            "user_id": request.user_id,
        },
    ) as root_span:
        with tracer.start_as_current_span("rag.retrieve") as rag_span:
            docs = retrieve_documents(request.query)
            rag_span.set_attribute("docs_retrieved", len(docs))

        with tracer.start_as_current_span(
            "llm.completion",
            attributes={"provider": "openai", "model": request.model},
        ) as llm_span:
            response = call_llm_provider(request, docs)
            llm_span.set_attribute("completion_tokens", response.token_count)
            llm_span.set_attribute("finish_reason", response.finish_reason)

        with tracer.start_as_current_span("safety.check") as safety_span:
            safe = check_safety(response.text)
            safety_span.set_attribute("passed", safe)
            if not safe:
                root_span.set_attribute("safety.violation", True)

        return response

During an incident, paste the trace ID from a failing request into Jaeger or Tempo to see exactly where latency is being added. This eliminates "is it us or the provider?" debates in under two minutes.
PagerDuty (or equivalent) is the authoritative source for on-call schedules and escalation paths. Configure it so the incident management tool can read from it automatically:

# PagerDuty service configuration
services:
  inference-api:
    escalation_policy: ml-platform-escalation
    alert_grouping:
      type: intelligent  # groups related alerts into one incident
      timeout: 300       # 5-minute grouping window

escalation_policies:
  ml-platform-escalation:
    rules:
      - escalation_delay_in_minutes: 5
        targets: [ml-platform-oncall]     # primary on-call
      - escalation_delay_in_minutes: 15
        targets: [ml-platform-secondary]  # secondary
      - escalation_delay_in_minutes: 30
        targets: [eng-leadership]         # engineering lead

# For SEV1: trigger high-urgency immediately
# For SEV2: trigger low-urgency, normal business hours override

Platforms like Incident.io, Rootly, and FireHydrant integrate Slack, PagerDuty, Jira, and status pages into a single lifecycle manager. Their key benefit is eliminating the manual steps that cause delay at T+0:
Triggered by PagerDuty webhook in under 5 seconds
Platform prompts IC with a UI dropdown
Scribe copies from a template Notion page
Platform creates it from a template with incident data pre-filled
Platform creates Jira tickets from /inc-action slash commands
Someone assembles the timeline from memory
Platform generates a draft post-mortem from the scribe log and timeline
Platform tracks MTTD, MTTR, incident frequency automatically
Runbooks stored in Confluence wiki pages drift. They are edited ad hoc, not reviewed, and often out of date by the time they are needed.
— Markdown files in the same Git repository as the service they document — are versioned, PR-reviewed, and linked directly from alert definitions:

# Prometheus alert with runbook link
- alert: InferenceAPIHighLatency
  expr: histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(inference_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) > 5
  for: 5m
  labels:
    severity: page
    team: ml-platform
  annotations:
    summary: "Inference API P99 latency > 5s"
    description: "P99 latency is {{ $value }}s for the past 5 minutes."
    runbook_url: "https://github.com/org/repo/blob/main/runbooks/inference-api-latency.md"
    dashboard_url: "https://grafana.internal/d/inference-overview"
When the alert fires, the on-call responder sees the runbook link immediately in PagerDuty and Slack. No searching required.
Every runbook for a GenAI system should follow this structure:

# Inference API High Latency

## Alert
`InferenceAPIHighLatency` — P99 > 5s for 5 minutes

## Impact
Users experience slow or timing-out responses. RAG pipelines may cascade fail.

## Triage (do these first, in order)
1. Check [Inference API dashboard](https://grafana.internal/d/inference-overview) — which region is affected?
2. Check [LLM Provider Health dashboard] — is the provider returning 429 or 503?
3. Run: `kubectl get pods -n inference | grep -v Running`
4. Check recent deploys: `kubectl rollout history deployment/inference-api -n inference`

## Mitigations
### Provider rate limiting (429 errors > 5%)
```bash
# Switch to backup provider
kubectl set env deployment/inference-api LLM_PROVIDER=anthropic -n inference
kubectl rollout status deployment/inference-api -n inference
```

### OOM on inference pods
```bash
# Cordon affected node, pods reschedule to healthy nodes
kubectl cordon <node-name>
kubectl drain <node-name> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
```

### Recent deploy introduced regression
```bash
kubectl rollout undo deployment/inference-api -n inference
```

## Escalation
If none of the above resolves within 20 minutes, page the ML Platform lead.

## Related alerts
- `LLMProviderErrorRate` — provider-side issues
- `InferencePodOOMKilled` — memory pressure
Create this as a Notion or Confluence template. The bot or scribe instantiates it at T+10 with the incident ID pre-filled:

# INC-{{incident_id}} — {{title}}

**Severity:** {{severity}} | **IC:** {{ic}} | **Scribe:** {{scribe}}
**Declared:** {{declared_time}} UTC | **Bridge:** {{bridge_url}}

---

## Current Status
**Hypothesis:** (scribe updates every 10 min)
**Confidence:** Low / Medium / High
**User impact:** (how many users, which regions, what they see)

## Actions In Flight
| Who | What | ETA | Status |
| --- | ---- | --- | ------ |
| | | | |

## Resolution Criteria
- [ ] (define before starting mitigation — what must be true to call this resolved?)

## Timeline
| Time UTC | Event |
| -------- | ----- |
| {{declared_time}} | Incident declared |
During an incident, responders need access to systems they may not use day-to-day. Pre-provision these — not during the incident:
Run this checklist monthly as part of your on-call rotation handoff:

#!/bin/bash
# war-room-readiness-check.sh
set -e

echo '=== War Room Readiness Check ==='

# 1. PagerDuty: verify on-call schedule is populated
echo 'Checking PagerDuty on-call...'
curl -s -H 'Authorization: Token token=$PD_TOKEN' \
  'https://api.pagerduty.com/oncalls?escalation_policy_ids[]=POLICY_ID' \
  | jq '.oncalls | length' | grep -q '^[1-9]' && echo 'OK' || echo 'FAIL: no on-call found'

# 2. Grafana: verify inference dashboard is reachable
echo 'Checking Grafana...'
curl -sf -o /dev/null 'https://grafana.internal/d/inference-overview' \
  && echo 'OK' || echo 'FAIL: Grafana unreachable'

# 3. Slack: verify incident bot is online
echo 'Checking Slack bot...'
curl -s 'https://slack.com/api/auth.test' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer $SLACK_BOT_TOKEN' \
  | jq '.ok' | grep -q 'true' && echo 'OK' || echo 'FAIL: Slack bot offline'

# 4. Statuspage: verify API key is valid
echo 'Checking Statuspage API...'
curl -sf -o /dev/null \
  -H 'Authorization: OAuth $STATUSPAGE_TOKEN' \
  'https://api.statuspage.io/v1/pages' && echo 'OK' || echo 'FAIL: Statuspage API key invalid'

echo '=== Check complete ==='
Responders use 5 different channels/tools during the same incident
Pick one coordination channel (Slack) and make everything else link from it
Responder spends 10 minutes writing a PromQL query during SEV1
Build and test dashboards in advance; link from alert annotations
Runbook references a service that was renamed 6 months ago
Runbooks as Markdown in the repo, reviewed in the same PR as the service change
Check on-call coverage weekly; alert-on-no-oncall as a separate alert
Responder wastes 3 minutes installing Zoom at 3 AM
Use browser-based bridge or pre-install on all engineer laptops as standard
Notion/Confluence template instantiated by a bot at T+0 with data pre-filled
The war room tooling stack has four layers: coordination, observability, incident management, and documentation
Slack is the coordination hub — all other tools link from it; slash commands speed up the scribe workflow
Pre-build dashboards for Inference API, LLM provider health, RAG pipeline, cost, and safety before any incident occurs
Distributed tracing (Jaeger/Tempo) with LLM spans lets responders identify latency sources in under two minutes
PagerDuty or equivalent must have intelligent alert grouping and tested escalation policies with no schedule gaps
Dedicated incident platforms (Incident.io, Rootly) eliminate manual T+0 friction and generate post-mortem drafts automatically
Runbooks as code in Git — linked from alert annotations — stay in sync with the system and are PR-reviewed
Run a monthly war room readiness check to verify all tools are operational before you need them
